subject: Physics
q: A radioactive nucleus decays by two different process. The half life of the first process is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> minutes and that of the second process is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. The effective half-life of the nucleus is calculated to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>α</mi><mn>11</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> is ______.
question: A radioactive nucleus decays by two different process. The half life of the first process is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> minutes and that of the second process is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. The effective half-life of the nucleus is calculated to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>α</mi><mn>11</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> is ______.
solution:
Decay law for both process can be written as,

dN1dt=-λ1N   ...Eq(1) and

dN2dt=-λ2N   ...Eq(2)

Hence, net decay will become

dNdt=dN1dt+dN2dt=-(λ1+λ2)N

⇒λeq=λ1+λ2

⇒ln2t12=ln2300+ln230

⇒t12=30011

Hence, required value of α=300.

The half life of the radioactive substance expressed in days is ________.
solution:
N0⟶t12N02⟶t12N04⟶t12N08⟶t12N016

4×t12=80

t12=20 days

q: Binding energy of a certain nucleus is $18 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~J}$. How much is the difference between total mass of all the nucleons and nuclear mass of the given nucleus:
question: Binding energy of a certain nucleus is $18 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~J}$. How much is the difference between total mass of all the nucleons and nuclear mass of the given nucleus:
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \Delta \mathrm{mc}^2=18 \times 10^8 \\ & \Delta \mathrm{m} \times 9 \times 10^{16}=18 \times 10^8 \\ & \Delta \mathrm{m}=2 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~kg}=20 \mu \mathrm{g}\end{aligned}$

When <math><mn>1</mn><mi> c</mi><msup><mrow><mn>m</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> blood is drawn from the animal's body after <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></math> of injection, the activity was found to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi>dpm</mi></math>(decays per minute), then the total volume of blood in the animal's body is close to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>[</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Ci</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>.7</mo><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>10</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>dps</mi></math>(decays per second) and at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></math> the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mi>λ</mi><mi>t</mi></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>0.84</mn><mo>]</mo></math>
solution:
Let the total volume of blood is v, initial activity A0=0.Q μCi its activity at time t,  A= A o e −λt , the activity of x volume,  A1=AVX=XAoVe-λt
⇒V=XA0A1e-λt⇒V=1 cm38×10-7×3.7×101030060 0.84
=4.97×103 cm2=4.97 liter=5 L.

solution:
124 mv2+12180 m4v1802=5.5 MeV

⇒  124 mv21+4541802=5.5 MeV

Another substance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> has atomic mass number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>32</mn></math> and half life of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math> day. If both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> simultaneously start undergo radio activity at the same time with initial mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>320</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> each, how many total atoms of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> combined would be left after <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> days
question: Substance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> has atomic mass number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn></math> and half life of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> day. Another substance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> has atomic mass number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>32</mn></math> and half life of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math> day. If both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> simultaneously start undergo radio activity at the same time with initial mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>320</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> each, how many total atoms of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> combined would be left after <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> days
solution:
Number of nuclei left after n half-lives is given by,

N=N012tt12.

As, N0A=32016=20 moles and 

N0B=32032=10 moles

Then, NA=N0A221=204=5 and 

NB=N0B220.5=1024=0.625

So total N = 5.625

 and number of atoms =5.625×6.023×1023

=3.38×1024

subject: Physics
q: For a nucleus of mass number $A$ and radius $R$, the mass density of nucleus can be represented as
question: For a nucleus of mass number $A$ and radius $R$, the mass density of nucleus can be represented as
solution:
The mass density ( $\rho$) of a nucleus is defined as the ratio of its mass to its volume:
$\rho=$ Mass of nucleus / Volume of nucleus
For a spherical nucleus with radius R , the volume is:
$\text { Volume }=(4 / 3) \pi R^3$
The mass of the nucleus is related to the mass number A :
$\text { Mass }-\mathrm{A} \times \mathrm{m} \text { _u }$
(where m_u is the atomic mass unit, approximately $1.66 \times 10^{-27} \mathrm{~kg}$)
Therefore:
$\rho=\left(\mathrm{A} \times \mathrm{m} \_\mathrm{u}\right) /\left[(4 / 3) \pi \mathrm{R}^3\right]$
Now, there's an important relationship between the mass number A and nuclear radius R. Empirically, it has been found that:
$\mathrm{R}=\mathrm{Ro} * \mathrm{~A}^{\wedge}(1 / 3)$
Where Ro is a constant approximately equal to $1.2 \times 10^{-15} \mathrm{~m}(1.2$ fermi).
Substituting this into the density equation:
$\begin{aligned}
& \rho=\left(\mathrm{A} \times \mathrm{m} \_\mathrm{u}\right) /\left[(4 / 3) \pi\left(\mathrm{Ro} \times \mathrm{A}^{\wedge}(1 / 3)\right)^3\right] \\ & \rho=(\mathrm{A} \times \mathrm{m} \text { _u }) /\left[(4 / 3) \pi \mathrm{Ro}^3 \times \mathrm{A}\right] \\ & \rho=\mathrm{m} \_\mathrm{u} /\left[(4 / 3) \pi \mathrm{Ro}^3\right]
\end{aligned}$
This shows that the nuclear density is approximately constant for all nuclei, regardless of their mass number. This is a fundamental property of nuclear matter known as nuclear saturation density.
The numerical value is:
$\rho=2.3 \times 10^{17} \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$

subject: Physics
q: A nucleus with mass number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>242</mn></math> and binding energy per nucleon as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MeV</mi></math> breaks into two fragment each with mass number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>121</mn></math>. If each fragment nucleus has binding energy per nucleon as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MeV</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the total gain in binding energy is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mo> </mo><mi>MeV</mi></math>.
question: A nucleus with mass number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>242</mn></math> and binding energy per nucleon as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MeV</mi></math> breaks into two fragment each with mass number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>121</mn></math>. If each fragment nucleus has binding energy per nucleon as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MeV</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the total gain in binding energy is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mo> </mo><mi>MeV</mi></math>.
solution:
Binding energy is given by E=∆mc2

where ∆m is the mass defect.

The energy per nucleon of the nucleus having mass number 242 is 7.6 MeV.

The initial binding energy is,

BE=242×7.6 MeV

The energy per nucleon of the nucleus with mass number 121 is 8.1 MeV.

Therefore, binding energy is

BE'=2121×8.1 MeV

The gain in the binding energy is 

BE'-BE = (8.1 – 7.6) × 242 MeV=121 MeV

= 121 MeV

subject: Physics
q:
Consider the following statements:
A. Atoms of each element emit characteristics spectrum.
B. According to Bohr's Postulate, an electron in a hydrogen atom revolves in a certain stationary orbit.

C. The density of nuclear matter depends on the size of the nucleus.
D. A free neutron is stable but a free proton decay is possible.
E. Radioactivity is an indication of the instability of nuclei. Choose the correct answer from the options given below.
question:
Consider the following statements:
A. Atoms of each element emit characteristics spectrum.
B. According to Bohr's Postulate, an electron in a hydrogen atom revolves in a certain stationary orbit.

C. The density of nuclear matter depends on the size of the nucleus.
D. A free neutron is stable but a free proton decay is possible.
E. Radioactivity is an indication of the instability of nuclei. Choose the correct answer from the options given below.
solution:
(A) True, an atom of each element emits a characteristic spectrum.

(B) True, according to Bohr's postulates mvr=nh2π and hence electron resides into orbits of specific radius called stationary orbits.

(C) False, the density of the nucleus is constant.

(D) False, A free neutron is unstable decays into proton and electron and antineutrino.

(E) True unstable nucleus show radioactivity.

question:

solution:
The value of Q is 

Q=(mA-mB-mD)×931.5 MeV⇒Q=(238.05079-234.04363-4.00260)×931.5 MeV⇒Q=4.25 MeV

subject: Physics
q: A free neutron decays into a proton but a free proton does not decay into neutron. This is because
question: A free neutron decays into a proton but a free proton does not decay into neutron. This is because
options: neutron is a composite particle made of a proton and an electron
neutron has larger rest mass than proton
solution:
Mass of the neutron is mn=1.67493×10-27 kg,

mass of the proton is mp=1.67262×10-27 kg.

Proton's rest mass is less than that of a neutron's rest mass. As particle can not decay to a state of greater mass, hence the proton does not decay to neutron outside nucleus whereas the neutron can decay into a proton though β-decay.

q:
The energy released per fission of nucleus of X240 is 200 MeV. The energy released if all the atoms in 120 g of pure X240 undergo fission is _____×1025 MeV.  

(Given NA=6×1023)
question:
The energy released per fission of nucleus of X240 is 200 MeV. The energy released if all the atoms in 120 g of pure X240 undergo fission is _____×1025 MeV.  

(Given NA=6×1023)
solution:
Energy released per fission is 200 MeV.

The number of atoms n in 120 g:

n=120240×6×1023 atoms.

Total energy released, E=n×200×106

=6×1025 MeV

q: A Sample of radioactive material <math><mi>A</mi><mo>,</mo></math> that has an activity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi><mo> </mo><mi>Ci</mi><mi> </mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mi> </mi><mi>C</mi><mi>i</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3.7</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>10</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> has twice the number of nuclei as another sample of a different radioactive material <math><mi>B</mi></math> which has an activity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi><mo> </mo><mi>Ci</mi></math>. The correct choices for half-lives of <math><mi>A</mi></math> and <math><mi>B</mi></math> would then be, respectively:
question: A Sample of radioactive material <math><mi>A</mi><mo>,</mo></math> that has an activity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi><mo> </mo><mi>Ci</mi><mi> </mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mi> </mi><mi>C</mi><mi>i</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3.7</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>10</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> has twice the number of nuclei as another sample of a different radioactive material <math><mi>B</mi></math> which has an activity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi><mo> </mo><mi>Ci</mi></math>. The correct choices for half-lives of <math><mi>A</mi></math> and <math><mi>B</mi></math> would then be, respectively:
solution:
Activity A=λN
10=λANA
20=λBNB
12=λAλB2
λAλB=14 ⇒TATB=41

subject: Physics
q: A nucleus of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> emits <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>γ</mi></math>-ray photon of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi></math>. The loss of internal energy by the nucleus is: [Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi></math> as the speed of electromagnetic wave]
question: A nucleus of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> emits <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>γ</mi></math>-ray photon of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi></math>. The loss of internal energy by the nucleus is: [Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi></math> as the speed of electromagnetic wave]
solution:
Energy of γ ray Eγ=hv

Momentum of γ ray Pγ=hλ=hvc
Total momentum is conserved.

P→γ+P→Nu=0
where P→Nu= momentum of decayed nuclei

⇒Pγ=PNu
⇒hvc=PNu
⇒KE of nuclei

=12Mv2=PNu22M=12Mhvc2
Loss in internal energy =Eγ+KENu
=hv+12Mhvc2
=hv1+hv2Mc2

subject: Physics
q: A radioactive nucleus <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> with a half-life <math><mi>T</mi></math>, decays into a nucleus <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math>. At <math><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, there is no nucleus <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math>. At some time <math><mi>t</mi></math>, the ratio of the number of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> to that of <math><mi>A</mi></math> is <math><mn>0</mn><mtext>.</mtext><mn>3</mn></math>. Then, <math><mi>t</mi></math> is given by: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mtext>Consider</mtext><mo> </mo><msub><mi>log</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mi>log</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced></mrow></mfenced></math>
question: A radioactive nucleus <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> with a half-life <math><mi>T</mi></math>, decays into a nucleus <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math>. At <math><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, there is no nucleus <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math>. At some time <math><mi>t</mi></math>, the ratio of the number of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> to that of <math><mi>A</mi></math> is <math><mn>0</mn><mtext>.</mtext><mn>3</mn></math>. Then, <math><mi>t</mi></math> is given by: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mtext>Consider</mtext><mo> </mo><msub><mi>log</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mi>log</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:
Let N A and N B be the number of molecules of A and B after time t.

Also, after time t, NBNA=0.3(given).

Also, let N 0 be the total number of nucleus initial

After time t, N A + N B = N 0

N A +0.3 N A = N 0

∴   N A = N 0 1.3

Also, the rate of disintegration of A

N A = N 0 e −λt

⇒ N 0 1.3 = N 0 e −λt ;  1 1.3 = e −λt    or  log( 1.3 )=λt

t=log(1.3)λ

∴t=Tlog(1.3)log(2)=Tlog(1.3)log2.

subject: Physics
q:
From the statements given below :

(A) The angular momentum of an electron in nth orbit is an integral multiple of h.

(B) Nuclear forces do not obey inverse square law.

(C) Nuclear forces are spin dependent.

(D) Nuclear forces are central and charge independent.

(E) Stability of nucleus is inversely proportional to the value of packing fraction.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
From the statements given below :

(A) The angular momentum of an electron in nth orbit is an integral multiple of h.

(B) Nuclear forces do not obey inverse square law.

(C) Nuclear forces are spin dependent.

(D) Nuclear forces are central and charge independent.

(E) Stability of nucleus is inversely proportional to the value of packing fraction.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
(A) Electron revolves around nucleus only in those orbits for which the angular momentum is an integral multiple of h2π.

(B) Nuclear force does not follow the inverse square law.

(C) Nuclear forces are spin dependent but charge independent.

(D) Nuclear force is attractive in nature. It is a non central force, meaning it does not depend upon the distance between the particles but rather upon their orientation. Nuclear force is stronger if the spin is parallel; and weak if it is anti parallel.

(E) The negative value (less quantity) of packing fraction indicates that the isotope is stable. Positive value of packing fraction implies unstable (radioactive) nucleus. Also, the stability of the nucleus is inversely proportional to the value of the packing fraction.

subject: Physics
q: The decay constant for a radioactive nuclide is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Atomic weight of the substance is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mole</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>N</mi><mi>A</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>23</mn></msup></math>. The activity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mo>µ</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of the substance is ________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>10</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Bq</mi></math>.
question: The decay constant for a radioactive nuclide is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Atomic weight of the substance is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mole</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>N</mi><mi>A</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>23</mn></msup></math>. The activity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mo>µ</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of the substance is ________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>10</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Bq</mi></math>.
solution:
It is given that 

λ=1.5×10-5 s-1

Number of moles is 1×10-660=10-76

Number of atoms=number of moles ×NA

N0=10-76×6×1023=1016

Hence, at t=0, A=A0=N0λ, the activity can be given by

A=N0λe-λt⇒A=1016×1.5×10-5×1=15×1010 Bq

subject: Physics
q: Two deuterons undergo nuclear fusion to form a Helium nucleus. The energy released in this process is (given binding energy per nucleon for deuteron<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mtext>1.1</mtext><mo> </mo><mi>MeV</mi></math> and for helium<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mtext>7.0</mtext><mo> </mo><mi>MeV</mi></math>)
question: Two deuterons undergo nuclear fusion to form a Helium nucleus. The energy released in this process is (given binding energy per nucleon for deuteron<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mtext>1.1</mtext><mo> </mo><mi>MeV</mi></math> and for helium<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mtext>7.0</mtext><mo> </mo><mi>MeV</mi></math>)
solution:
Q=∑B.E. of products- ∑B.E. of reactants

The equation for two deuterons combining to form Helium nucleus is given as H2+1H2→2H4 

Energy released=Q=4B.E.2H4 -4B.E.1H2  
 =4×7−4×1.1

=28−4.4

The energy released in this process is =23.6 MeV

subject: Physics
q: $7.9 \mathrm{MeV} \alpha$-particle scatters from a target material of atomic number 79. From the given data the estimated diameter of nuclei of the target material is (approximately) $\_\_\_\_$ m. $\left[\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{\mathrm{o}}}=9 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{Nm}^{2} / \mathrm{C}^{2}\right.$ and electron charge $\left.=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}\right]$
question: $7.9 \mathrm{MeV} \alpha$-particle scatters from a target material of atomic number 79. From the given data the estimated diameter of nuclei of the target material is (approximately) $\_\_\_\_$ m. $\left[\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{\mathrm{o}}}=9 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{Nm}^{2} / \mathrm{C}^{2}\right.$ and electron charge $\left.=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}\right]$
solution:
For Rutherford scattering, at the closest approach all kinetic energy converts to electrostatic potential energy.
For alpha particle-nucleus collision:
$K.E. = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\frac{Z_1 Z_2 e^2}{r_{min}}$
Where: $Z_1 = 2$ (alpha), $Z_2 = 79$ (gold), $K.E. = 7.9$ MeV
$r_{min} = \frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 2 \times 79 \times (1.6 \times 10^{-19})^2}{7.9 \times 10^6 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}$
$r_{min} = 2.88 \times 10^{-14}$ m
The estimated nuclear diameter is approximately:
$d = 2 \times r_{min} = 5.76 \times 10^{-14}$ m

Then:
question: The half life period of a radioactive element <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is same as the mean life time of another radioactive element <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Initially they have the same number of atoms. Then:
options: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math> decay at the same rate always.

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math> have same decay rate initially and later on different decay rate.
solution:
The formula of half-life in terms of the decay constant, t12=0.693λx=1λy
⇒λx=0.693λy⇒ λx<λy

subject: Physics
q: If three helium nuclei combine to form a carbon nucleus then the energy released in this reaction is _____$\times 10^{-2} \mathrm{MeV}$. (Given $1 \mathrm{u}=931 \mathrm{MeV} / \mathrm{c}^2$, atomic mass of helium $=4.002603 \mathrm{u}$ )
question: If three helium nuclei combine to form a carbon nucleus then the energy released in this reaction is _____$\times 10^{-2} \mathrm{MeV}$. (Given $1 \mathrm{u}=931 \mathrm{MeV} / \mathrm{c}^2$, atomic mass of helium $=4.002603 \mathrm{u}$ )
solution:
Reaction :
$\begin{aligned}
& 3{ }_2^4 \mathrm{He} \longrightarrow{ }_6^{12} \mathrm{C}+\gamma \text { rays } \\
& \text { Mass defect }=\Delta \mathrm{m}=\left(3 \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{He}}-\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{C}}\right) \\
& =(3 \times 4.002603-12)=0.007809 \mathrm{u}
\end{aligned}$
Energy released
$=931 \Delta \mathrm{m} \mathrm{MeV}$
$=7.27 \mathrm{MeV}=727 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{MeV}$

subject: Physics
q: The half-life of a radioactive nucleus is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> years. The fraction of the original sample that would decay in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn></math> years is :
question: The half-life of a radioactive nucleus is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> years. The fraction of the original sample that would decay in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn></math> years is :
solution:
The formula to calculate the number of half lives covered by the radioactive material is given by

n=tT12   ...1

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (1) to calculate the number of half lives covered.

n=15 years5 years= 3

The relation between the amount of radioactive substance after certain time can be written as

N=N02-n   ...2

Substitute the value of n into equation (2) to obtain the fraction of the remaining substance.

N=N02-3⇒NN0=18

Hence, the amount of the decayed substance can be written as

1-NN0=1-18=78

Assertion (A) : The binding energy per nucleon is found to be practically independent of the atomic number A, for nuclei with mass numbers between 30 and 170.
Reason (R): Nuclear force is long range.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements. One is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).
Assertion (A) : The binding energy per nucleon is found to be practically independent of the atomic number A, for nuclei with mass numbers between 30 and 170.
Reason (R): Nuclear force is long range.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options: Both $(\mathbf{A})$ and $(\mathbf{R})$ are true and $(\mathbf{R})$ is the correct explanation of $(\mathbf{A})$
Both (A) and (R) are true but (R) is NOT the correct explanation of (A)
solution:
From graph between B.E/N & A we can see BE/N is almost constant $\Rightarrow$ correct
Reason $\Rightarrow$ incorrect as nuclear forces are short range forces.

q: The half-life of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi>Au</mi><mprescripts></mprescripts><none></none><mn>198</mn></mmultiscripts></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> days. If atomic weight of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi>Au</mi><mprescripts></mprescripts><none></none><mn>198</mn></mmultiscripts></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>198</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, then the activity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mg</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi>Au</mi><mprescripts></mprescripts><none></none><mn>198</mn></mmultiscripts></math> is [in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>disintegration</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>second</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>]:
question: The half-life of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi>Au</mi><mprescripts></mprescripts><none></none><mn>198</mn></mmultiscripts></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> days. If atomic weight of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi>Au</mi><mprescripts></mprescripts><none></none><mn>198</mn></mmultiscripts></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>198</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, then the activity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mg</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi>Au</mi><mprescripts></mprescripts><none></none><mn>198</mn></mmultiscripts></math> is [in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>disintegration</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>second</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>]:
solution:
A=λN

λ=ln2t1/2=ln23×24×60×60 sec-1=2.67×10-6 sec-1

N= Number of atoms in 2 mg Au

=2×10-3198×6×1023=6.06×1015

A=λN=1.618×1013=16.18×1012 dps

subject: Physics
q:
When Uranium is bombarded with neutrons, it undergoes fission. The fission reaction can be written as :
${ }_{92} \mathrm{U}^{235}+{ }_0 n^1 \rightarrow{ }_{56} \mathrm{Ba}^{141}+{ }_{36} \mathrm{Kr}^{92}+3 x+\mathrm{Q}($ energy)
where three particles named $x$ are produced and energy $\mathrm{Q}$ is released. What is the name of the particle $x$ ?
question:
When Uranium is bombarded with neutrons, it undergoes fission. The fission reaction can be written as :
${ }_{92} \mathrm{U}^{235}+{ }_0 n^1 \rightarrow{ }_{56} \mathrm{Ba}^{141}+{ }_{36} \mathrm{Kr}^{92}+3 x+\mathrm{Q}($ energy)
where three particles named $x$ are produced and energy $\mathrm{Q}$ is released. What is the name of the particle $x$ ?
solution:
Nuclear fission equation
$\begin{aligned}
{ }_{92} \mathrm{U}^{135}+{ }_0 \mathrm{n}^1 \longrightarrow & { }_{56} \mathrm{Ba}^{141}+{ }_{36} \mathrm{Kr}^{92} \\
& +3{ }_0 \mathrm{n}^1+\mathrm{Q}(\text { energy })
\end{aligned}$
Hence particle $\mathrm{x}$ is neutron.

subject: Physics
q:
A piece of wood from a recently cut tree shows 20 decays per minute. A wooden piece of the same size placed in a museum (obtained from a tree cut many years back) shows 2 decays per minute. If the half-life of C14 is 5730 years, then the age of the wooden piece placed in the museum is approximately
[This question was awarded a bonus and proper correction was made to avoid that]
question:
A piece of wood from a recently cut tree shows 20 decays per minute. A wooden piece of the same size placed in a museum (obtained from a tree cut many years back) shows 2 decays per minute. If the half-life of C14 is 5730 years, then the age of the wooden piece placed in the museum is approximately
[This question was awarded a bonus and proper correction was made to avoid that]
solution:
We know that,

The rate of decay at the time t is

A=A0e-λt

2=20·e-λt

∴ 110=e-λt

∴ eλt=10

⇒lneλt=ln10

⇒λt=ln10

∵ λ=ln2T1/2

t=ln10ln2T1/2

∵ ln10=2.303 and ln2=0.693

t=2.3030.693×5730

=3.32×5730

≈19042 years
